retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Ustekinumab is a human monoclonal antibody typically used to treat moderate to severe plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, moderate to severe Crohn disease, or moderate to severe ulcerative colitis (inflammatory bowel disease). Ustekinumab mediates the body's T-cell response by acting as an antagonist against interleukin-12 (IL12) and interleukin-23 (IL23). The drug is FDA approved for use in moderate to severe plaque psoriasis (Ps), particularly in those patients who are candidates for phototherapy or systemic therapy, aged 6 or older. Patients with active psoriatic arthritis (PsA) may use ustekinumab as monotherapy or in combination with methotrexate.

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ustekinumab is a medication used to manage and treat a variety of inflammatory conditions, including psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease. It is in the monoclonal antibody class of medications. This activity reviews the indications, action, and contraindications for ustekinumab as a valuable agent in treating inflammatory diseases. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors (e.g., off-label uses, dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, monitoring, relevant interactions) pertinent for members of the interprofessional team in the management of psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Administration -- Dosage and Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis treatment are based on an initial intravenous weight-based infusion followed by a subcutaneous maintenance schedule. [22] [23] Initial infusion dosage: Weight < 55 kg: 260 mg (2 vials) Weight 55 to 85 kg : 390 mg (3 vials) Weight > 85 kg: 520 mg (4 vials) Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis recommended adult S/C maintenance dosage: 90 mg subcutaneously eight weeks after initial intravenous administration and every eight weeks after that. Renal dosing (including both hemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis) and hepatic dosing are undefined.

retrieved_docs.title: Psoriatic Arthritis -- Treatment Planning -- Ustekinumab (IL12/23i)
retrieved_docs.content: Human mab to IL-12/23 p40 subunit Dose: 45 mg SC every 3 months after loading (90 mg if weight >100 kg) FDA-approved for psoriatic arthritis, psoriasis, and Crohn’s disease

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Contraindications -- Caution Recommended
retrieved_docs.content: Latex hypersensitivity (with the pre-filled syringe dose form) Patients aged over 60 Chronic or recurrent infections Latent tuberculosis or those with TB risk Malignancy of a history of malignancy prolonged immunosuppressant therapy Allergen immunotherapy Photochemotherapy (PUVA)

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain the mechanism of action of ustekinumab. Describe the indications for the use of ustekinumab. Review the potential adverse event that can accompany ustekinumab use. Outline strategies utilized by healthcare teams to improve care coordination and communication when ustekinumab is being administered. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Bispecific Antibody Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Toxicity with these biologics can occur in the prehospital or in-hospital setting. Individuals on bsAbs may present unconscious, apneic, and pulseless. Airway, breathing, and circulation must be immediately assessed, and resuscitation should be performed promptly for individuals on cardiorespiratory arrest. A more detailed evaluation may be pursued once the patient is hemodynamically stable.

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Adverse Effects -- Common Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Nasopharyngitis Headache Fatigue Vomiting after induction in inflammatory bowel disease Injection site erythema Bronchitis/upper respiratory tract infection Sinusitis Abdominal pain, fever, influenza, and diarrhea while used for maintenance therapy in inflammatory bowel disease Back pain

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Administration -- Dosage and Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Psoriasis adult S/C recommended dosage: Less than or equal to 100 kg: 45 mg administered subcutaneously initially, followed by another injection four weeks later and then every 12 weeks Greater than 100 kg: 90 mg administered subcutaneously initially, followed by another injection four weeks later and then every 12 weeks. [18] Psoriatic arthritis adult S/C recommended dosage: Less than or equal to 100 kg: 45 mg administered subcutaneously initially, followed by another injection four weeks later and then every 12 weeks Greater than 100 kg with co-existent moderate-severe plaque psoriasis: 90mg administered subcutaneously initially, followed by another injection four weeks later and then every 12 weeks. [19] [20] Psoriasis pediatric patients recommended subcutaneous dosage: Doses are administered initially, four weeks later, and then every 12 weeks. [21] Weight < 60 kg: 0.75 mg/kg subcutaneously Weight 60 to 100 kg: 45 mg subcutaneously Weight > 100 kg: 90 mg subcutaneously

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Contraindications -- Absolute Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Hypersensitivity to ustekinumab or any of the excipients Active infection Active tuberculosis

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: There have been reports of the rapid development of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma in those patients treated with ustekinumab. These patients often had risk factors for developing this type of cancer, such as being older (greater than 60) and having undergone phototherapy or other immunosuppressant treatment. Patients should be monitored closely for the development of non-melanoma skin cancers. [37] As with all biological drugs, live vaccines should not be administered while on ustekinumab treatment. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Administration -- Ulcerative Colitis
retrieved_docs.content: The initial dose of golimumab is 200 mg (subcutaneous). At week 2, 100 mg (subcutaneous), followed by 100 mg every 4 weeks

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: The patients must be monitored clinically for the development of severe infections, including TB, jaundice, malignancy, non-infectious pneumonia, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome, or serious skin conditions such as exfoliative dermatitis or erythrodermic psoriasis. Patients additionally should be encouraged to report any concerning symptoms. Routine blood tests, including full blood count, urea, electrolytes, liver function tests, and inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR), should be checked approximately every 4 to 6 months. [32] [33] Clinical monitoring for efficacy should be carried out using disease-specific measuring systems for psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease. Re-testing for hepatitis and HIV can be considered if clinically indicated. There are experimental data to suggest that monitoring serum drug levels may be beneficial during ustekinumab treatment, but this is not the current standard of care. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2151
retrieved_docs.content: Antiastmatika, a class of anti‑asthmatic agents, includes betamethason as a specific member of this therapeutic group. Although primarily recognized for its anti‑asthmatic properties, betamethason is also employed in the management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), illustrating its dual utility in both respiratory and dermatologic treatment. This overlap underscores the importance for clinicians to consider betamethason’s broader therapeutic spectrum when addressing patients who may have concurrent asthma and psoriasis, thereby supporting more comprehensive and individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2127
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a diverse group of agents that dampen immune activity and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune diseases, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. This class includes inhibitory interleukin compounds, the antimetabolite mycophenolate and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil, antilymphocyte serum, azaserin, the monoclonal antibodies basiliximab and daclizumab, the mTOR inhibitor everolimus, and the fungal toxin gliotoxin. Additional members are cyclophosphamide (koformycin), mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), the glucocorticoid paramethasone, and the calcineurin inhibitor tacrolimus. These drugs act through various mechanisms—such as blocking interleukin signaling, inhibiting nucleotide synthesis, antagonizing the IL‑2 receptor, inhibiting mTOR signaling, or modulating glucocorticoid and calcineurin pathways—to achieve immunosuppression and improve patient outcomes in settings where controlling the immune response is critical.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1460
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a broad pharmacologic class that includes a diverse array of agents used to attenuate immune system activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. Within this class are inhibitory interleukinu compounds, which target interleukin pathways to reduce immune activation, and mycophenolate, a purine synthesis inhibitor that limits lymphocyte proliferation. Antilymfocytární sérum, a polyclonal antibody preparation, also belongs to the immunosuppressant group, as do azaserin and basiliximab, both of which interfere with T‑cell activation. Cyklosporiny (cyclosporine) and takrolimus (tacrolimus) are calcineurin inhibitors that suppress cytokine production, while daklizumab (daclizumab) blocks the IL‑2 receptor to prevent T‑cell expansion. Additional members include elipticiny, gliotoxin, and the pro‑drug hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu (mycophenolate mofetil hydrochloride), which is metabolized to the active agent mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová). Koformycin, a nucleoside analog, and muromonab‑CD3, a monoclonal antibody directed against the CD3 complex, further expand the repertoire of immunosuppressive therapies. Collectively, these agents illustrate the extensive range of mechanisms—such as cytokine inhibition, lymphocyte depletion, and signal transduction blockade—through which immunosuppressants achieve therapeutic immune modulation, underscoring their critical role in contemporary patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorization indicates that each of these agents is a systemic therapy used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Although they share the same therapeutic class, the drugs differ in their mechanisms of action: amlexanox is a small‑molecule agent, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target interleukin‑5, and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody directed against thymic stromal lymphopoietin. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their common role in managing severe or refractory obstructive airway disease, while highlighting the variety of pharmacologic strategies available within this therapeutic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 687
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants represent a broad class of agents that dampen immune activity through a variety of mechanisms and are employed in transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. The group includes inhibitory interleukin agents, antimetabolites such as mycophenolate, mycophenolate mofetil, mycophenolate mofetil hydrochloride, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), azaserin, and koformycin, which interfere with nucleotide synthesis and lymphocyte proliferation. Calcineurin inhibitors, exemplified by cyklosporiny (cyclosporine) and takrolimus (tacrolimus), block T‑cell activation by inhibiting calcineurin signaling. The mTOR pathway is targeted by everolimus, providing another route to suppress cell growth and immune responses. Monoclonal antibodies and biologic agents such as basiliximab, daklizumab (daclizumab), muromonab‑CD3, and elipticiny act on specific surface receptors or cytokine pathways to prevent lymphocyte activation. Polyclonal preparations like antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum) also contribute to immunosuppression by broadly neutralizing immune cells. Glucocorticoids, including meprednisone and paramethason (paramethasone), exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects by altering gene transcription. Additionally, gliotoxin, a fungal toxin, possesses immunosuppressive properties through inhibition of NF‑κB and other signaling cascades. Together, these diverse agents illustrate the multiple biochemical targets—interleukin signaling, calcineurin, mTOR, nucleotide synthesis, surface receptors, and transcriptional pathways—through which immune function can be attenuated, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate regimens and managing associated risks.

retrieved_docs.content: adrenergic and anticholinergic inhalants, corticosteroid combinations, antiallergic agents, systemic bronchodilators, and biologic monoclonal antibodies—provide a structured framework that supports clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacologic strategies for patients with obstructive airway diseases and related inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a broad category of medications that includes antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents used in combination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1737
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants constitute a therapeutic class that dampens immune system activity and are employed in transplant rejection prophylaxis, autoimmune disease management, and other conditions requiring immune modulation. Within this class, basiliximab, everolimus and gliotoxin are recognized as specific immunosuppressive agents. Additional agents classified as immunosuppressants include koformycin, the mycophenolic acid derivative known as kyselina mykofenolová, the corticosteroid meprednisone, the monoclonal antibody muromonab‑CD3, the corticosteroid paramethason, and the calcineurin inhibitor takrolimus. The concept of an inhibitory interleukin is also considered a subordinate element of the immunosuppressant category, reflecting that certain interleukin‑targeting drugs function as immune‑suppressing therapies. Together, these drugs and mechanisms illustrate the breadth of pharmacologic strategies used to control immune responses in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 381
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants comprise a diverse group of agents that are used to dampen immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. This class includes inhibitory interleukin agents, the antimetabolite mycophenolate (also known as mycophenolic acid) and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil (including its hydrochloride form), as well as azaserin, basiliximab, cyclosporine, everolimus, gliotoxin, muromonab‑CD3, and tacrolimus. Mycophenolate and mycophenolic acid function by blocking purine synthesis in lymphocytes, whereas cyclosporine and tacrolimus inhibit calcineurin, preventing activation of T‑cell transcription factors. Everolimus exerts its effect through inhibition of the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway, limiting cell proliferation. Basiliximab and muromonab‑CD3 are monoclonal antibodies that target specific surface receptors on activated T cells, providing targeted immunosuppression. Gliotoxin, a fungal toxin, also possesses immunosuppressive properties through interference with cytokine signaling. Collectively, these agents illustrate the multiple mechanistic pathways—interleukin inhibition, antimetabolite activity, calcineurin blockade, mTOR inhibition, and monoclonal antibody‑mediated receptor blockade—by which immunosuppressants achieve therapeutic immune modulation, underscoring their central role in contemporary patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690
retrieved_docs.content: Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason is classified as a member of the group “Corticosteroids acting locally,” indicating that it is a locally acting corticosteroid formulation. Because of its potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, betamethason is commonly employed in the therapeutic management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), an inflammatory skin disorder. The drug’s ability to reduce skin inflammation and plaque formation makes it an effective topical option for patients with psoriasis, highlighting the relevance of locally acting corticosteroids in dermatologic care.

retrieved_docs.content: pathways of antimetabolite‑related agents—antilymphocyte serum, azaserin, basiliximab, and basiliximab’s monoclonal‑antibody counterpart daklizumab—are qualified by the presence of cysteine, indicating a role for this amino acid in their synthesis. Immunosuppressants constitute a therapeutic subclass of immunological factors and of koformycin‑related agents. This subclass includes azathioprine sodium, antilymphocyte serum, azaserin, basiliximab, cyclosporines, daklizumab, ellipticines, everolimus, gliotoxin, hydrochloride mycophenolate mofetil, mycophenolate, mykofenolát mofetil, and inhibitory interleukin, as well as mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), muromonab‑CD3, paramethasone, and tacrolimus. These agents act to diminish immune activity in transplant or autoimmune settings. Biosynthesis, a core cellular process for the formation of complex molecules, can be qualified, described, or modulated by a diverse set of chemical and biological entities. Fungal metabolites

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants represent a diverse pharmacologic class that includes antilymphocyte serum, azaserine, cyclosporine, daclizumab, ellipticins, everolimus, gliotoxin, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), muromonab‑CD3, and tacrolimus (takrolimus). Within this class, inhibitory interleukin agents are considered a subordinate subgroup (“has child”). These agents act to dampen immune responses and are employed in conditions requiring suppression of lymphocyte activity, such as organ transplantation, autoimmune diseases, and certain inflammatory disorders. However, the use of immunosuppressants is contraindicated in the setting of human herpesvirus 1 (HSV‑1) infection, reflecting the heightened risk of viral reactivation when immune function is deliberately reduced. Recognizing both the therapeutic breadth of immunosuppressants and their specific contraindications is essential for safe patient management and optimal clinical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1690
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants comprise a diverse group of agents that are used to dampen immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. The class includes mycophenolate and its pro‑drug mykofenolát mofetil (mycophenolate mofetil), as well as the hydrochloride form of mykofenolát mofetilu, which all act by inhibiting inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase and thereby reducing lymphocyte proliferation. Kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid) is the active acid form of this pathway and is likewise classified as an immunosuppressant. Takrolimus (tacrolimus) and koformycin are calcineurin‑inhibiting and nucleoside‑analog agents, respectively, that suppress T‑cell activation through distinct molecular mechanisms. Inhibitory interleukinu (interleukin inhibitors) represent biologic immunosuppressants that block cytokine signaling pathways, while daclizumab (daklizumab) is a monoclonal antibody targeting the IL‑2 receptor to prevent lymphocyte expansion. Antilymfocytární sérum (antilymphocyte serum) provides polyclonal antibodies that deplete circulating lymphocytes, and azaserin is another antibody‑based agent with immunosuppressive properties. Elipticiny (ellipticins) and gliotoxin are natural‑product‑derived compounds that exhibit immunosuppressive activity through mechanisms such as inhibition of NF‑κB signaling and induction of oxidative stress. Together, these agents—mycophenolate, mykofenolát mofetil, hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu, kyselina mykofenolová, takrolimus, koformycin, inhibitory interleukinu, daclizumab, antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, elipticiny, and gliotoxin—form a comprehensive pharmacologic arsenal for controlling undesirable immune responses, each offering specific advantages and considerations for individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 39
retrieved_docs.content: blockers, alanyl‑glutamine (a dipeptide), and other dipeptides are recognized for nutritional and therapeutic roles. Vitamins such as fat‑soluble vitamin K are essential for coagulation, while copper sulfate and benzodiazepine antagonists (sharing the antidote category with ipecac) serve as antidotes. Antithrombotic enzymes of the plasmin system—plasmin, protein C, streptokinase, tissue‑type plasminogen activator (tPA, also classified as an ophthalmological agent), anistreplasa, and tenekteplaza—participate in fibrinolysis. Protein C and abciximab (a monoclonal antibody) are also linked to enzyme inhibition; protein C inhibition is a subcategory of “inhibition of protein synthesis.” Monoclonal antibodies in the therapeutic armamentarium include abciximab, basiliximab, and daclizumab. The immunosuppressant family is extensive: cyclosporin (enzyme inhibitor), tacrolimus, everolimus, mycophenolate mofetil hydrochloride, mycophenolic acid, azaserin, basiliximab, daclizumab, gliotoxin,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1393
retrieved_docs.content: Antilymfocytární sérum and basiliximab are both products of biosynthesis, and the biosynthetic processes that generate these agents can be qualified or modulated by a variety of biochemical and pharmacological factors. Cystein, protoveratriny, and trisacharidy serve as molecular substrates or regulators that influence the enzymatic pathways of biosynthesis. Immunological factors, including inhibitory interleukinu, further shape the biosynthetic environment by modulating immune‑related signaling cascades. Therapeutic monoclonal antibodies such as daclizumab and muromonab‑CD3, as well as the nucleoside analogue koformycin, are recognized as agents that can affect biosynthetic activity, either by targeting specific cellular targets or by altering metabolic pathways. Additionally, testosterone propionate (testosteronpropionát) is noted to impact biosynthetic mechanisms, reflecting its role in hormone‑driven protein synthesis. Together, these entities illustrate the complex network of substrates, regulators, and pharmacologic agents that define and qualify the biosynthesis of immunologically active products like antilymfocytární sérum and basiliximab.
